headline: A Simple Cut: How Removing Vines Revives Borneo's Forests
meta_description: Researchers in Borneo discover that cutting invasive lianas triples forest recovery speed compared to tree planting alone, offering hope for restoring logged f…
summary_short: A large-scale experiment in Malaysian Borneo reveals that simply cutting invasive vines accelerates forest regeneration three times faster than planting saplings.
body:
In the race to restore Earth's degraded forests, scientists have discovered an unexpectedly powerful tool: a machete.

A new study in Malaysian Borneo finds that cutting lianas—fast-growing woody vines that overtake disturbed forests—allows regenerating woodland to recover three times faster than through tree planting alone. Over nearly two decades, researchers monitored 500 hectares of secondary forest, comparing plots where saplings were planted, plots where saplings were planted and lianas were cut, and untreated control areas.

The results were striking. When freed from their liana burden, forests added an extra 27 centimeters of canopy height each year and accumulated carbon at triple the rate of enrichment planting by itself. Lidar technology revealed why: trees grew faster and experienced lower mortality rates when vines no longer strangled them for water, nutrients, and sunlight.

What makes this discovery particularly valuable is its practicality. Liana cutting cost as much as 10 times less than planting saplings, making it an accessible option even for resource-limited conservation efforts. For lightly logged forests like those in the study area, cutting vines alone may provide the jumpstart needed for natural regeneration.

As climate change and biodiversity loss intensify, this simple intervention offers tangible hope—and proof that sometimes the most effective solutions are refreshingly straightforward.
